title: Smoke and Oxygen Physics
steps: In a fire, heat and toxic gases rise. The highest concentration of carbon monoxide and superheated air is near the ceiling.
To survive, you must stay as low to the ground as possible, ideally crawling below 60cm (2 feet).
If you must breathe through smoke, use a cloth (preferably wet) over your nose and mouth to filter out large particulate matter, though be aware it will not stop carbon monoxide.

title: Airflow and Backdrafts
steps: Fire requires oxygen to survive. Opening a door or breaking a window can create a sudden influx of oxygen.
This influx can lead to an explosive fire growth known as a backdraft.
Never break a window unless you are immediately escaping through it.
Always feel a door with the back of your hand before opening; if it is hot, fire is on the other side. Do not open it.

title: Compartmentalization and Isolation
steps: A closed solid door can hold back a raging fire for 15-20 minutes or more.
If trapped, your goal is to isolate yourself. Close all doors between you and the fire.
Seal the bottom cracks with wet towels, duct tape, or heavy clothing to prevent smoke from seeping in.
Cover air vents if smoke is coming through them.

title: Heat Radiation and Conduction
steps: Radiant heat can ignite materials from a distance.
If you are near a large fire, protect your skin by covering it with heavy, natural fibers like wool or thick cotton.
Synthetic fibers (like polyester or nylon) will melt onto your skin and cause severe burns.
If trapped in a room, stay away from the walls nearest the fire.

title: Flashover Awareness
steps: A flashover occurs when all combustible materials in a room simultaneously ignite due to extreme radiant heat (usually around 500-600°C at the ceiling).
If you notice the smoke layer banking down quickly, or if the heat becomes suddenly unbearable, a flashover is imminent.
You have seconds to evacuate the room or close the door.
